words.shortDefinition: Automatic Reference Counting; Swift's memory management system.
words.simpleDefinition: Automatic memory management — when nothing references an object anymore, Swift deallocates it automatically. No garbage collector pauses.
words.longDefinition: Automatically tracks and manages your app's memory usage. ARC automatically frees up the memory used by class instances when those instances are no longer needed, using a count of references to determine when an object can be safely deallocated.
words.connector: Think of ARC like library checkout — as long as someone has the book checked out (a reference), it stays. When everyone returns it (zero references), the library removes it from circulation.
words.techContext: Unlike garbage collection in Java, ARC happens at compile-time by inserting retain/release calls, which results in more predictable performance.
words.exampleSentence: We had a memory leak in our navigation flow because two objects were holding 'strong' references to each other, preventing ARC from cleaning them up.

words.shortDefinition: A process for querying and calling properties, methods, and subscripts on an optional that might be nil.
words.simpleDefinition: Use '?' to safely drill into nested objects — if any part is nil, the whole chain returns nil instead of crashing.
words.longDefinition: If the optional contains a value, the property/method call succeeds; if the optional is nil, the property/method call returns nil. This allows you to fail gracefully without checking for nil at every level of a hierarchy.
words.connector: Think of optional chaining like dominoes — if one tile is missing (nil), the chain stops there instead of falling over (crashing).
words.techContext: We used optional chaining to access the user's city name (user?.address?.city) to avoid crashing if the user didn't have an address stored.
words.exampleSentence: Optional chaining makes our networking code much cleaner by handling potentially missing data in a single line.
words.etymology: from 'optional' (something that may or may not exist) + 'chaining'.

codeExample.code:
let streetName = john.residence?.address?.street
// If residence or address is nil, streetName is nil

words.shortDefinition: Self-contained blocks of functionality that can be passed around and used in your code.
words.simpleDefinition: Swift's version of anonymous functions — a block of code you can pass around like a variable, and it remembers the context where it was created.
words.longDefinition: Closures in Swift are similar to lambdas in other languages. They can capture and store references to any constants and variables from the context in which they are defined. This is known as 'closing over' those constants and variables.
words.connector: Think of closures like a note you write that includes a reminder of where you were when you wrote it — the code plus its context.
words.techContext: We passed a closure to the network request function to handle the response data once the download finished.
words.exampleSentence: I used a trailing closure to define the animation that should run when the button is tapped.

words.shortDefinition: A design pattern that allows one object to send messages to another object when an event happens.
words.simpleDefinition: One object says 'I'll do the work, but you decide what happens when stuff changes' — hands off control via protocol methods.
words.longDefinition: A fundamental pattern in iOS development. One object (the delegator) keeps a reference to another object (the delegate) and calls methods on it to notify it of changes or to ask for data. The delegate typically conforms to a specific protocol.
words.connector: Think of delegation like a secretary screening calls — they answer the phone but hand you the important ones to decide what to do.
words.techContext: We implemented the UITableViewDelegate to handle what happens when a user taps on a row in our settings screen.
words.exampleSentence: I set the view controller as the delegate for the text field so I could be notified when the user finishes typing.
words.etymology: from Latin delegare ('to send as a representative').

words.shortDefinition: A blueprint of methods, properties, and other requirements that suit a particular task or piece of functionality.
words.simpleDefinition: A contract — if you adopt this protocol, you promise to implement these methods and properties. Like an interface in Java.
words.longDefinition: Similar to interfaces in other languages. A class, struct, or enum can 'adopt' a protocol to provide an actual implementation of those requirements. Protocols are the foundation of 'Protocol Oriented Programming' in Swift.
words.connector: Think of protocols like a job description — it lists what you need to do, but you figure out how to actually do it.
words.techContext: We created a 'Searchable' protocol so that any of our data models could be used with the new global search feature.
words.exampleSentence: By conforming to the Codable protocol, our data model can be automatically converted to and from JSON.
words.etymology: from Greek prōtokollon ('first leaf glued into a manuscript').

words.shortDefinition: Apple's modern declarative framework for building user interfaces across all Apple platforms.
words.simpleDefinition: Apple's React-like UI framework — describe what you want, and SwiftUI keeps it in sync with your data automatically.
words.longDefinition: Introduced in 2019, it allows developers to define UI using simple Swift code. SwiftUI automatically keeps your views in sync with your data, using a reactive approach similar to React or Flutter.
words.connector: Think of SwiftUI like a spreadsheet formula — change the data, the UI updates itself. No manual DOM manipulation.
words.techContext: We used SwiftUI to build the new dashboard because it allowed us to create a responsive layout for both iPhone and iPad with 70% less code.
words.exampleSentence: SwiftUI's live previews allowed me to see the UI changes instantly without building and running the app in the simulator.

words.shortDefinition: The classic imperative framework for building iOS user interfaces.
words.simpleDefinition: The old-school iOS UI framework — manually create views, wire up delegates, update UI with code. Before SwiftUI existed.
words.longDefinition: Provides the crucial infrastructure for iOS and tvOS apps, including the window and view architecture, event handling, and common UI components like buttons, tables, and navigation controllers.
words.connector: Think of UIKit like jQuery — you directly manipulate the UI step by step, vs SwiftUI's React-like declarative approach.
words.techContext: While we're moving to SwiftUI for new features, our legacy screens still use UIKit and UIViewController.
words.exampleSentence: I had to use a UIViewRepresentable to wrap an older UIKit chart library so we could use it inside our new SwiftUI view.

words.shortDefinition: A mechanism for adding a layer of separation between code that manages how a property is stored and code that defines a property.
words.simpleDefinition: Add '@Something' before a property to get special behavior — @State triggers UI updates, @Published notifies observers, @AppStorage saves to UserDefaults.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to reuse management logic for properties. In SwiftUI, wrappers like @State, @Binding, and @Environment are used to manage the flow of data and trigger UI updates when data changes.
words.connector: Think of property wrappers like gift wrapping — the property is the gift, the @ wrapper adds extra behavior around it.
words.techContext: We used the @AppStorage property wrapper to automatically persist the user's 'dark mode' preference to UserDefaults.
words.exampleSentence: The @Published property wrapper in our ViewModel automatically notifies the UI whenever the data array is updated.

words.shortDefinition: A modern way to write asynchronous code in Swift that looks like synchronous code.
words.simpleDefinition: Write async code that reads top-to-bottom like normal code — 'await' pauses the function until the result arrives, no callbacks needed.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to write code that performs long-running tasks (like network calls) without using complex completion handlers or closures. It makes asynchronous code easier to read, write, and debug.
words.connector: Think of async/await like checkpoints in a game — code pauses at 'await', waits for the result, then continues from exactly where it left off.
words.techContext: We refactored our network layer from closures to async/await to eliminate 'callback hell' and make error handling more natural with try-catch.
words.exampleSentence: Using await ensures that the function pauses until the data is fetched, but it doesn't block the main thread.

words.shortDefinition: Adds new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
words.simpleDefinition: Add methods to types you don't own — extend String, Int, even Apple's classes, without touching the original source code.
words.longDefinition: Extensions in Swift are powerful because they allow you to add methods and properties to types for which you do not have the source code (like Apple's String or UIColor). They are also used to organize code into logical blocks.
words.connector: Think of extensions like adding a new app to your phone — the phone didn't ship with it, but now it can do new things.
words.techContext: I wrote an extension for Double to easily format currencies throughout the entire app.
words.exampleSentence: We used an extension on the Date type to add a helper method that returns 'Today', 'Yesterday', or a formatted string.

words.shortDefinition: A value type in Swift used to encapsulate data and related behavior.
words.simpleDefinition: A value type — gets copied when you pass it around. Safer than classes because each copy is independent, no shared state to worry about.
words.longDefinition: Unlike classes (which are reference types), structs are copied when they are passed around in your code. They are generally faster and safer because they avoid shared state, and they are the primary building blocks of SwiftUI.
words.connector: Think of structs like photocopies — editing your copy doesn't change anyone else's copy, unlike classes which are like shared Google Docs.
words.techContext: Apple recommends using structs by default and only switching to classes when you specifically need identity or inheritance.
words.exampleSentence: We defined our 'Product' model as a struct to ensure that each view gets its own independent copy of the data.

words.shortDefinition: Apple's framework for processing values over time.
words.simpleDefinition: Apple's reactive programming framework — Publishers send values over time, Subscribers react. Like RxSwift but first-party.
words.longDefinition: A declarative Swift API for handling asynchronous events by combining event-processing operators. It is similar to RxSwift. It allows you to create 'Publishers' that emit values and 'Subscribers' that react to them.
words.connector: Think of Combine like a river — values flow downstream through operators (map, filter, debounce) until they reach subscribers at the end.
words.techContext: We used Combine to link the search text field to the API call, using the 'debounce' operator to avoid hitting the server too frequently.
words.exampleSentence: Combine made it easy to synchronize the state of multiple toggles in our settings menu.
words.etymology: from Latin combinare ('to join two by two').

words.shortDefinition: Grand Central Dispatch; a low-level API for managing concurrent operations.
words.simpleDefinition: Apple's threading API — run code on background queues to keep the UI responsive, then jump back to the main queue to update the UI.
words.longDefinition: Also known as Dispatch. It allows you to execute blocks of code on different queues (Main, Global, Custom). It handles the creation and management of threads behind the scenes to maximize performance.
words.connector: Think of GCD like a factory with multiple assembly lines — offload heavy work to background lines, then send finished products to the main line for display.
words.techContext: We used GCD to offload the heavy data processing to a background queue so the UI wouldn't freeze while the app was busy.
words.exampleSentence: Remember to use DispatchQueue.main.async when you need to update the UI from a background task.

words.shortDefinition: A globally unique actor that performs its tasks on the main thread.
words.simpleDefinition: Mark code with @MainActor to guarantee it runs on the main thread — critical for UI updates in Swift concurrency.
words.longDefinition: In modern Swift concurrency, the @MainActor attribute is used to ensure that a class or function always executes on the main thread. This is critical for UI updates, which must always happen on the main thread.
words.connector: Think of @MainActor like a VIP pass — guarantees the code always runs on the main thread, no threading bugs.
words.techContext: I marked the ViewModel with @MainActor to guarantee that all its published property updates are thread-safe and safe for SwiftUI to consume.
words.exampleSentence: The compiler gave me a warning because I tried to update a view property from a function that wasn't marked as @MainActor.

words.shortDefinition: A protocol indicating that a type is safe to share across concurrency boundaries.
words.simpleDefinition: A marker that says 'this type can be safely passed between threads/actors' — the compiler checks to prevent data races.
words.longDefinition: Types that conform to Sendable can be safely passed from one task or actor to another without risking data races. Value types like Structs and Enums are implicitly Sendable if all their properties are Sendable.
words.connector: Think of Sendable like tamper-proof packaging — the compiler verifies the contents can survive shipping between threads without breaking.
words.techContext: We had to make our custom Error type conform to Sendable so it could be thrown from a background task to the main thread.
words.exampleSentence: The Swift compiler helps prevent crashes by ensuring that non-Sendable classes aren't shared across different threads.

words.shortDefinition: Apple's native tool for managing the distribution of Swift code.
words.simpleDefinition: Apple's official dependency manager — built into Xcode, add libraries with a URL, no CocoaPods or Carthage needed.
words.longDefinition: Abbreviated as SPM. It is integrated into the Swift build system and Xcode, allowing you to easily add, remove, and update third-party libraries (dependencies) in your project using a Package.swift file.
words.connector: Think of SPM like npm or pip — manages your third-party libraries, but first-party and deeply integrated into the tooling.
words.techContext: We switched from CocoaPods to SPM to manage our project dependencies because it's much faster and better integrated into Xcode.
words.exampleSentence: I added the Alamofire library to our project using the Swift Package Manager in just a few clicks.

words.shortDefinition: An enumeration that represents either a success or a failure.
words.simpleDefinition: An enum that's either .success(value) or .failure(error) — can't be both, can't be neither, forces you to handle both cases.
words.longDefinition: It is often used in completion handlers for asynchronous operations. It provides a standardized way to return data or an error, making it impossible to accidentally return both or neither.
words.connector: Think of Result like a coin flip — it's heads (success) or tails (failure), never both, never neither.
words.techContext: The API client returns a 'Result<User, NetworkError>', forcing the caller to handle the error case explicitly.
words.exampleSentence: I used a switch statement on the result to update the UI if the login was successful, or show an alert if it failed.

words.shortDefinition: A Swift feature that allows enums to store extra information along with their case.
words.simpleDefinition: Enum cases can carry data — like .success(User) or .error(String), not just plain tags.
words.longDefinition: Unlike simple enums, Swift enums can store values of any type. For example, a 'Status' enum might have a '.failed' case that stores a specific Error object explaining why it failed.
words.connector: Think of associated values like an envelope with a label — the case is the label (success/failure), the associated value is what's inside the envelope.
words.techContext: We used an enum with associated values to represent the state of our network request: .idle, .loading, .success(Data), and .error(String).
words.exampleSentence: Associated values make enums incredibly flexible for modeling complex states in our application.

words.shortDefinition: A type that hides its concrete identity from the caller.
words.simpleDefinition: Return 'some Protocol' instead of the exact type — caller knows it conforms, compiler knows the real type, best of both worlds.
words.longDefinition: Uses the 'some' keyword. It allows a function to return a value that conforms to a protocol without revealing the specific class or struct behind it. This is used in SwiftUI to return 'some View'.
words.connector: Think of opaque types like a mystery box labeled 'contains fruit' — you know it's fruit, but not whether it's an apple or orange until you open it.
words.techContext: The 'body' property of a SwiftUI view returns 'some View', allowing the compiler to optimize the layout while keeping the code simple.
words.exampleSentence: By returning an opaque type, we can change our view hierarchy without breaking the code that calls the function.

words.shortDefinition: Apple's framework for writing unit, performance, and UI tests for Apple platforms.
words.simpleDefinition: Apple's built-in testing framework — write unit tests, UI tests, performance tests, all integrated into Xcode.
words.longDefinition: Provides a comprehensive set of tools to verify that your code works as expected. It is fully integrated with the Xcode Test Navigator and CI systems like Xcode Cloud.
words.connector: Think of XCTest like JUnit or pytest — assertions, test cases, test suites, but Apple-flavored and Xcode-integrated.
words.techContext: I wrote an XCTest case to verify that the currency conversion logic handles negative numbers correctly.
words.exampleSentence: The build failed in CI because two XCTest assertions failed after the latest code change.

words.shortDefinition: Code that can work with any type, subject to requirements that you define.
words.simpleDefinition: Write one function that works with any type — func sort<T>() instead of sortInts(), sortStrings(), sortDates().
words.longDefinition: Allows you to write flexible, reusable functions and types that can work with any type. This avoids code duplication and provides type safety at the same time. Array and Dictionary are the most common examples of generics in Swift.
words.connector: Think of generics like a universal charger — one adapter works with any device, vs carrying a different cable for each gadget.
words.techContext: We created a generic 'ResourceFetcher' class that can download and decode any type that conforms to Codable.
words.exampleSentence: Using generics allowed us to write a single function for sorting both numbers and strings.
words.etymology: from Latin genericus ('belonging to a genus').

words.shortDefinition: Model-View-ViewModel; an architectural pattern commonly used in iOS apps.
words.simpleDefinition: Three-layer architecture — View (UI), ViewModel (presentation logic), Model (data). ViewModel sits between View and Model, keeps them decoupled.
words.longDefinition: Separates the UI (View) from the data (Model) using an intermediary (ViewModel). The ViewModel handles the presentation logic and interacts with the Model, providing formatted data to the View.
words.connector: Think of MVVM like a restaurant — View is the dining room, Model is the kitchen, ViewModel is the waiter shuttling info between them.
words.techContext: We adopted MVVM in our SwiftUI app to make the logic easier to unit test independently of the UI.
words.exampleSentence: In our MVVM setup, the View observes the ViewModel for changes and updates itself automatically.

words.shortDefinition: A type that can represent either a value or the absence of a value (nil).
words.simpleDefinition: A value that might be nil — must unwrap it with 'if let' or 'guard let' before using, compiler won't let you skip it.
words.longDefinition: A core safety feature of Swift. You must explicitly unwrap an optional (using 'if let' or 'guard let') before you can access its value, preventing the 'null pointer exceptions' common in other languages.
words.connector: Think of optionals like a box that might be empty — you have to open it (unwrap) before you can use what's inside, if anything.
words.techContext: The 'middleName' property is an optional String because not everyone has a middle name.
words.exampleSentence: I used a 'guard let' statement to safely unwrap the optional username before displaying it in the profile.

words.shortDefinition: A visual representation of the user interface of an iOS application.
words.simpleDefinition: Drag-and-drop UI design in UIKit — layout screens and transitions visually instead of code. Nightmare to merge in git.
words.longDefinition: A file that allows you to design your UI by dragging and dropping components onto a canvas. It shows the flow between different screens (segues). While widely used in UIKit, it is not used in SwiftUI.
words.connector: Think of storyboards like Adobe XD for iOS — design the UI and navigation visually, Xcode generates the code behind it.
words.techContext: Merging changes to a shared storyboard file is often difficult, so our team prefers to split them into multiple smaller storyboards or use code-based UI.
words.exampleSentence: I added a new segue in the storyboard to connect the login button to the main dashboard.
words.etymology: from 'story' + 'board', borrowed from film and animation production.

words.shortDefinition: A property whose initial value isn't calculated until the first time it's used.
words.simpleDefinition: A property that doesn't initialize until you actually use it — saves startup time, defers expensive work until needed.
words.longDefinition: Used to delay the creation of expensive objects or to perform setup that requires the instance to be fully initialized first. You define them using the 'lazy' keyword.
words.connector: Think of lazy properties like a vending machine — the snack only drops when you press the button, not when you walk by.
words.techContext: I marked the database connection as a lazy property so the app starts up faster and only connects if it actually needs to perform a query.
words.exampleSentence: Using a lazy property for the heavy image filter improved the responsiveness of our list scrolling.

codeExample.code:
lazy var expensiveObject = ComplexSetup()
// Only initialized when first accessed
